inference_unit.notations: - $T_c$: Superconducting transition temperature - a.c. susceptibility: Alternating current magnetic susceptibility - $Tl_{2}Ca_{x}Ba_{y}Cu_{z}O_{8+w}$: Thallium-Calcium-Barium-Copper-Oxide chemical system

premises.premise: The presence of strong diamagnetic repulsion in a material is a physical property that serves as an experimental indicator of the existence of bulk superconductivity within that material.

The stable levitation of a material sample over a magnetic field (such as a field of approximately $5,000$ G) is a qualitative physical manifestation of strong diamagnetic repulsion, which is associated with the Meissner effect in superconductors.

The technique of Norton is an experimental method used to determine the temperature dependence of the alternating current (a.c.) susceptibility of a material sample to identify its superconducting transition characteristics. [15]

reasoning.step: A sample with nominal composition $Tl_{2}Ca_{2}BaCu_{3}O_{9+x}$ is immersed in liquid nitrogen and observed to levitate over a magnetic field of $\sim 5,000$ G.
The authors interpret the observed levitation as a demonstration of strong diamagnetic repulsion [@premise-2].
The authors assert that this strong diamagnetic repulsion is indicative of bulk superconductivity in the samples [@premise-1].
The temperature dependence of the a.c. susceptibility is measured for a sample of nominal composition $Tl_{2}Ca_{1.5}BaCu_{3}O_{8.5+x}$ using the technique of Norton [@premise-3].
The a.c. susceptibility measurements (showing deviation from zero slope) are used to confirm the superconducting nature of the samples.

conclusion.title: Verification of Bulk Superconductivity via Magnetic Response

inference_unit.conclusion: The Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O samples display strong diamagnetic repulsion indicative of bulk superconductivity, demonstrated by the levitation of a sample with nominal composition $Tl_{2}Ca_{2}BaCu_{3}O_{9+x}$ over a $\sim 5,000$ G magnetic field and confirmed by AC susceptibility measurements.
